evidence: The paper explicitly uses MDI (Gini importance): "The Mean Decrease in Impurity (MDI, or Gini importance) (Breiman, 2002)." They report MDI magnitude changes: "MDI increased with both sample size and number of variables." However, their main conclusions focus on target-based encoding bias to OOB estimates, not MDI cardinality bias. Critically, they acknowledge: "MDI is known to be biased in favour of variables with many possible split points (Strobl et al., 2007)" and recommend using permutation importance (MDA) instead: "calculate variable importance via MDA using an independent test set." They corroborate findings with permutation importance throughout and use held-out validation, so substantive conclusions do not depend on MDI magnitude/ranking.
